---
title: ""
author: "Giorgio Arcara"
date: ""
format:
  beamer:
    theme: metropolis
    keep-tex: false
incremental: false
knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    dev: pdf
header-includes:
  \setbeamerfont{title}{size=\large}
  \usepackage{hyperref}
  \setbeamertemplate{footline}[frame number]
  \usepackage{tikz}
  \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, positioning, shapes.geometric}
  \usetikzlibrary{overlay-beamer-styles}
---

```{=latex}
\title{{\fontsize{15pt}{16pt}\selectfont Applied Psychometrics}:\\ studying subclinical psychopathologies\\ \vspace{1.5em} \raggedright 
\emph{10.4 - Why Don't We Always Use Known-Groups?}}
\author{Giorgio Arcara,\\ University of Padua \\ IRCCS San Camillo, Venice}

\titlegraphic{
\vspace*{7cm}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{Figures/LogoSanCamilloIRCCS_Unipd_alpha.png}
\begin{center}
\vspace{-2.2em}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{Figures/CC_license_3_0.png}
\end{center}
}
\date{2026}
\maketitle
```

## A Reasonable Question

The known-group approach, comparing a clinical group to controls, gives us **lot of information**: sensitivity, specificity, ROC curves, PPV, and NPV.

\vfill

Despite the limitations in interpreting probability, It is clearly \textbf{richer} than normative-based testing alone.

\vfill
\pause

So why don't we \emph{always} use it?
\pause
\vfill
\small
The short answer: because it requires something we do not always have: a \textbf{well-defined, discrete group} with the condition.

## Continuous Gradients vs Discrete Categories

```{r}
#| echo: FALSE
#| fig.width: 6.5
#| fig.height: 4.2
#| fig.dpi: 100

par(mfrow = c(2, 1), mar = c(2, 2, 2.5, 2))

# --- Upper panel: continuous gradient ---
n   <- 100
col_lo  <- col2rgb("#FFAC1C") / 255
col_hi  <- col2rgb("steelblue") / 255
grad    <- sapply(seq(0, 1, length.out = n), function(t)
  rgb(col_lo[1]*(1-t) + col_hi[1]*t,
      col_lo[2]*(1-t) + col_hi[2]*t,
      col_lo[3]*(1-t) + col_hi[3]*t))

image(matrix(1:n, ncol  = 1), col = grad,
      axes = FALSE, main = "Continuous variation\n(e.g. subclinical anxiety trait)",
      cex.main = 0.9)
mtext("Low", side = 1, at = 0.02, line = 0.3, cex = 0.8, col = "#FFAC1C", font = 2)
mtext("High", side = 1, at = 0.98, line = 0.3, cex = 0.8, col = "steelblue", font = 2)

# --- Lower panel: two discrete rectangles ---
plot(NULL, xlim = c(0, 1), ylim = c(0, 1), axes = FALSE,
     main = "Discrete categories\n(e.g. presence / absence of a diagnosis)",
     cex.main = 0.9)
rect(0.02, 0.1, 0.47, 0.9, col = "#FFAC1C", border = NA)
rect(0.53, 0.1, 0.98, 0.9, col = "steelblue", border = NA)
text(0.245, 0.50, "Condition", col = "white", font = 2, cex = 1.0)
text(0.755, 0.50, "No Condition",    col = "white", font = 2, cex = 1.0)
```

## When a "Known Group" Does Not Exist

The known-group approach requires a \textbf{clear, established boundary} between those who have the condition and those who do not.

This boundary is often absent or problematic:

## When boundaries may not be appropriate


\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Subclinical constructs}: anxiety, subclinical depression, impulsivity: these exist on a continuum in the general population, with no natural discrete cut-point.
\item \textbf{No agreed gold standard}: for many psychological constructs, there is no definitive diagnostic procedure to determine who "truly" belongs to the clinical group.
\item \textbf{Spectrum conditions}: autism, personality disorders; the boundary between clinical and non-clinical is gradual and contested.
\end{itemize}

## Summary

The known-group approach is powerful: but it presupposes a \textbf{discrete, identifiable condition}.

<!--
## Summary

\begin{itemize}
\item The known-group approach is powerful: but it presupposes a \textbf{discrete, identifiable condition}.
\item When the construct of interest is \textbf{dimensional} (a trait or continuum), there is no natural clinical group to recruit.
\item When there is \textbf{no agreed gold standard}, assembling a valid known group is impossible.
\item In these cases, normative-based testing: despite providing less information, remains the \textbf{only feasible option}.
\end{itemize}

\vfill
\small
The choice between approaches is not a statistical preference: it is determined by the \textbf{nature of the construct}.

-->
